content.text:
*"Ask and it will be given to you; seek and you will 
find."*{Matthew 7:7}{Sorting is a fundamental problem, and understanding it deeply leads to insights about algorithms in general.}{Donald Knuth}

Two of the most fundamental problems in computer science are **searching** 
(finding an element in a collection) and **sorting** (arranging 
elements in order). Understanding these algorithms provides insight into 
algorithm design generally.
The simplest search method examines each element in sequence.

content.title: Linear Search
content.content:
verbatim
 Algorithm LINEAR-SEARCH(A[1..n], target)
 for i from 1 to n do
 if A[i] = target then
 return i
 end if
 end for
 return NOT_FOUND
verbatim

content.text:
Linear search requires at most $n$ comparisons—it has **time 
complexity** $O(n)$.
If the array is **sorted**, we can search much more efficiently.

content.title: Binary Search
content.content:
verbatim
 Algorithm BINARY-SEARCH(A[1..n], target)
 // Precondition: A is sorted in ascending order
 lo <- 1
 hi <- n
 while lo <= hi do
 mid <- floor((lo + hi) / 2)
 if A[mid] = target then
 return mid
 else if A[mid] < target then
 lo <- mid + 1
 else
 hi <- mid - 1
 end if
 end while
 return NOT_FOUND
verbatim

content.text:
Binary search halves the search space at each step, giving time complexity 
$O(\log n)$—dramatically faster than linear search for large $n$.

**Bubble sort** repeatedly swaps adjacent elements 
that are out of order.

content.title: Bubble Sort
content.content:
verbatim
 Algorithm BUBBLE-SORT(A[1..n])
 for i from 1 to n-1 do
 for j from 1 to n-i do
 if A[j] > A[j+1] then
 swap A[j] and A[j+1]
 end if
 end for
 end for
verbatim

content.text: Bubble sort makes approximately $n^2/2$ comparisons, giving $O(n^2)$ time.

**Merge sort** uses divide-and-conquer: split the 
array in half, sort each half recursively, then merge the sorted halves.

content.title: Merge Sort (Conceptual)
content.content:
verbatim
 Algorithm MERGE-SORT(A[1..n])
 if n <= 1 then
 return A
 end if
 mid <- floor(n/2)
 L <- MERGE-SORT(A[1..mid])
 R <- MERGE-SORT(A[mid+1..n])
 return MERGE(L, R)
verbatim

content.text:
The MERGE procedure combines two sorted arrays into one sorted array in 
$O(n)$ time. The overall time complexity is $O(n \log n)$.

content.title: Comparison of Sorting Algorithms
content.content:
| Algorithm | Time (Worst) | Space |
| Bubble Sort | $O(n^2)$ | $O(1)$ |
| Insertion Sort | $O(n^2)$ | $O(1)$ |
| Merge Sort | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n)$ |
| Quick Sort | $O(n^2)$ | $O(\log n)$ |
content.label: Theorem

marginNotes.text:
It is the glory of God to conceal a matter; 
to search out a matter is the glory of kings.
